slides.heading: The Hangover That Won't Lift
bullet_points: A poem about love as intoxication
When the mind and heart stop agreeing
By Tarun Gupta
slides.speaker_note: Open with the title and frame the emotional premise — this is about losing your internal coordinates, not just losing someone.

slides.heading: It starts quietly
bullet_points: One day you're fine
The next, someone's laugh lives rent-free in your head
The part of your brain that runs the show has quietly handed over the keys
slides.speaker_note: Establish the hook — this isn't dramatic heartbreak, it's the slow occupation of mental bandwidth you didn't consent to.

slides.heading: The poem opens
bullet_points: Love has taken away all my senses / like that of alcohol
effects of booze reside for a short period
but your intoxication doesn't want to go away
slides.speaker_note: Read the opening stanza slowly — let the hangover metaphor land before moving on to what makes this intoxication different.

slides.heading: What makes it stay
bullet_points: Vague longing is forgettable
But twinkling eyes, ebony hair — that's a face. A real one.
Specificity is what the mind cannot release
slides.speaker_note: Key insight: named details stick in a way abstractions never do — the poem doesn't try to universalize, and that's exactly why it lands.

slides.heading: When love feels like physics
bullet_points: like a moth attracted to the flame
like waves to the shore
like cold to the winter / like a rainbow after the rain
slides.speaker_note: Stack these four images — they escalate from something drawn to something inevitable, no deliberation anywhere in the chain.

slides.heading: The collision
bullet_points: Earlier, mind and heart worked in sync
Then: their ecosystems have collided
No ground to stand on. No sky to cover my head with.
slides.speaker_note: This is the emotional center — not chaos, not obsession, just the sudden disorienting loss of the coordinates you always relied on.

slides.heading: Not a malfunction
bullet_points: When the system is overturned, that's a signal
The only honest one you'll get that something actually matters
That's not a problem to solve. That's the experience itself.
slides.speaker_note: Close with the reframe — losing your coordinates isn't weakness, it's evidence of something real enough to overturn the whole architecture.
